commonIssues: Susceptible to fungal diseases (anthracnose, botrytis, root rot) and to cane borers and crown borers; ensure good air circulation.
Intolerant of waterlogged or wet soils; standing water causes root rot and plant death, so well-drained soil or containers are essential.
Spreads aggressively by suckers into tangled thickets if not pruned and contained.

Container culture: although a deep-rooted plant best grown in-ground, dwarf/everbearing types grow in a pot at least 40cm (16in) wide and 30cm (12in) deep; in very cold winters insulate or move the pot under cover so roots do not freeze hard.
Growing-season tissue is frost-tender: summer shoots are killed near -8C even though dormant, acclimated canes tolerate far colder; protect new spring growth from late frosts.

source.citation: UMN Extension — Growing raspberries in the home garden: full sun for most fruit; 1 to 1.5 inches of water per week from flowering until harvest, roots stay in top two feet of soil; space red/yellow raspberries 2 to 3 feet apart; all raspberries require support; summer-bearing fruit early to mid summer; composted manure as nutrient source.
UMN Extension — Growing raspberries in the home garden: hardy varieties (Boyne, Latham) rated very good to good in USDA zones 3-4.
UMN Extension — Growing raspberries in the home garden: all types of raspberries require support to prevent canes from wind damage, bending and cracking.

RHS — How to grow raspberries: grow in a pot at least 40cm (16in) wide and 30cm (12in) deep for compact varieties; sunny site best (light shade reduces crop); slightly acidic soil ideally pH 6-6.7; container plants fed monthly with a liquid general-purpose fertiliser through the growing season; ground plants fed granular fertiliser in early spring; plant bare-root canes Nov-Mar; summer varieties crop Jun-Sep, autumn varieties Aug-Oct.
RHS — How to grow raspberries: keep container compost consistently moist throughout the growing season; water new plants well during prolonged dry spells in the first growing season.
RHS — How to grow raspberries: feed container raspberries with a liquid general-purpose fertiliser on a monthly basis; ground plants need granular feed in early spring at most.
RHS — How to grow raspberries: dormant planting window November to March in the UK climate.

Missouri Botanical Garden Plant Finder — Rubus idaeus: USDA hardiness zones 4 to 8; full sun to part shade; medium water needs; prefers moist but well-drained, organically rich, slightly acidic soils and is intolerant of wet conditions causing root rot; mature 3-9 ft; blooms Apr-May, summer ripening; susceptible to anthracnose, botrytis, root rot, cane and crown borers; spreads into thickets if unmaintained.

Penn State Extension — Small Fruit Cold Hardiness, Winter Injury in Brambles: in mid-summer raspberry shoots are killed at about 18F (about -8C); by mid-October acclimated plants withstand about 10F (about -12C); summer-bearing red raspberries are the most cold hardy bramble type.

Container size conversion: RHS recommends a pot at least 40cm wide x 30cm deep; a cylinder of those dimensions holds roughly 38 L, and the Old Farmer's Almanac recommends about 8 US gallons (~30 L) of potting mix for container berries. recommendedMinLiters set conservatively to 19 L (5 US gal) as a workable minimum for dwarf/everbearing types, with 30-38 L as the ideal. Raspberries are deep-rooted and grow best in-ground; the container minimum is intentionally a large pot.

Confidence note: heatStressC (30C) and hotWeatherMultiplier are estimated. Extension sources specify 1-1.5 in/week and irrigation three or more times per week on sandy soil in dry spells but give no explicit heat-stress temperature; baseIntervalDays=3 and frostSensitive=false (dormant canes hardy to zone 3-4 but growing-season tissue tender near -8C) are conservative interpretations rather than directly stated single numbers.
